scope: The GRADE-Stack reference agent: a single-agent planner→executor→validator that triages an inbound support email and drafts a structured reply, fronted by the LLM gateway, over a text-only provider seam.

statusLegend.covered: A named, shipped mechanism addresses this threat for the reference architecture; residual limits (if any) are stated.
statusLegend.partial: A named mechanism reduces this threat but leaves a stated residual gap a deployment must close.
statusLegend.gap: The reference architecture has no mechanism for this threat — explicitly flagged, never silently assumed safe.

scopingNote: Each threat also carries a `scored` flag. `scored: false` marks a threat that is out of architectural scope — it targets a capability this single-agent, stateless reference does not implement (a long-term memory store; an agent-to-agent channel). Such a threat is reported with its `residualGap` stating why the capability is absent, but is excluded from the weighted score's denominator: it is a scope boundary, NOT a deficiency to fix. The boundary becomes a real, scored obligation the moment a deployment adds that capability.

items.title: Agent Goal Hijack
items.summary: Attackers manipulate the agent's goals, plans, or decision paths through direct or indirect instruction injection, redirecting it to unintended objectives.

mechanisms.name: Gateway server-side prompt-injection / override denial
mechanisms.note: Override/jailbreak attempts are refused at the gateway regardless of the agent's own system prompt — proven in Phase 2C (the bypass prompt is blocked even when the agent is told to comply).

mechanisms.name: Gateway is the sole model path
mechanisms.note: The agent holds no provider credentials, so injected instructions cannot route around the guardrail to reach a model directly.

items.residualGap: Injection detection is heuristic (pattern-based); a novel obfuscated injection can evade it. This is defense-in-depth, not a guarantee — pair with least-privilege tool scoping for high-risk deployments.

items.title: Tool Misuse & Exploitation
items.summary: Agents misuse or abuse legitimate tools through unsafe composition, recursion, or excessive execution, causing harmful side effects despite valid permissions.

mechanisms.name: Name-blind tool selection
mechanisms.note: The model selects tools from descriptions + arg schemas only — never names or prompt rules — so a tool cannot be steered by naming or prompt-side coercion.

mechanisms.name: MCP tool-vs-resource control model
mechanisms.note: Model-controlled actions (tools) are separated from app-controlled data (resources), so reference data cannot be invoked as an action.

mechanisms.name: Enforced turn bound
mechanisms.note: Caps runaway tool-invocation loops; the bound throws rather than loops.

items.residualGap: The reference tools are read-only lookups; the stack has no per-tool authorization or side-effect sandbox. A deployment adding mutating tools must add least-privilege scoping and rate limits.

items.title: Agent Identity & Privilege Abuse
items.summary: Delegated authority, ambiguous agent identity, or trust assumptions lead to unauthorized actions, impersonation, or exploitation of cross-agent trust.

mechanisms.name: Structural credential isolation
mechanisms.note: The agent process holds zero provider credentials (sandbox factory + stripped env + dead-ended IMDS/credential files); the gateway alone is credentialed. Verified in Phase 2C — a direct-to-provider call from the agent process fails for lack of credentials.

items.residualGap: Complete for the single-agent reference architecture (the agent has no privileges to escalate). Multi-agent deployments with delegated identity must add per-agent identity and authority scoping — see ASI07.

items.title: Agentic Supply Chain Compromise
items.summary: External agents, tools, schemas, or prompts that the agent dynamically trusts or imports become compromised — including schema manipulation and tool-description deception.

mechanisms.name: CI secret scan (gitleaks) + pre-commit + PreToolUse hooks
mechanisms.note: Stops credentials entering the repo/supply chain; CI scan is the mechanism of record.

mechanisms.name: Pinned dependencies (committed Bun lockfile)
mechanisms.note: Dependency versions are pinned and reviewed, reducing drift-based compromise.

mechanisms.name: Name-blind selection resists tool-description deception
mechanisms.note: Selection is by description + schema; a deceptive tool name cannot bias routing.

items.residualGap: No automated software-composition / vulnerability scanning of dependencies, and no signature/provenance verification of MCP tools or schemas imported at runtime. A production deployment should add SCA and signed-tool verification.

items.title: Unexpected Code Execution
items.summary: Agent-generated or agent-triggered code executes without sufficient validation or isolation, enabling unauthorized code execution or command injection.

mechanisms.name: No code-execution surface (structural)
mechanisms.note: The reference agent never executes model-generated code — no eval, no shell, no code-exec tool. Model output is consumed only as structured text.

mechanisms.name: Validator Zod schema-parse, not prompted execution
mechanisms.note: Output is extracted and safeParse'd against a schema and rejected on mismatch — it is never interpreted as code.

items.residualGap: The attack surface is structurally absent for the reference agent. Any deployment that adds a code-execution or shell tool must add sandboxing/isolation before this remains covered.

items.title: Memory & Context Poisoning
items.summary: Injection or leakage into agent memory or contextual state corrupts future reasoning or actions through long-term memory poisoning or state manipulation.

mechanisms.name: Gateway input scanning (partial mitigation)
mechanisms.note: In-flight injected content is scanned, but this does not validate a persistent memory store.

items.residualGap: Flagged as a gap: the reference agent is stateless per run and has no long-term memory subsystem, so there is no memory provenance/validation mechanism. Any deployment that adds persistent memory or RAG state must add write-provenance, integrity checks, and context isolation — this stack does not provide them.

items.title: Insecure Inter-Agent Communication
items.summary: Manipulation of messages exchanged between agents, planners, and executors through interception, injection, or spoofing of agent-to-agent communication.

mechanisms.name: Single controlled network seam (agent → gateway over HTTP)
mechanisms.note: The only network boundary is the agent-to-gateway call, which is a controlled, policy-enforced channel — but this is not multi-agent (A2A) messaging.

items.residualGap: Flagged as a gap: the reference stack is single-agent (planner/executor/validator are in-process phases, not networked agents), so there is no inter-agent channel and therefore no A2A authentication, encryption, or message-integrity mechanism. Any multi-agent deployment must add authenticated, integrity-protected inter-agent messaging.

items.title: Cascading Agent Failures
items.summary: Small agent failures propagate through connected systems — tool-chain errors, resource-exhaustion cascades — causing large-scale impact.

mechanisms.name: Enforced turn bound + gateway token/model caps
mechanisms.note: Bounds runaway loops and per-request resource consumption, limiting blast radius.

mechanisms.name: Eval gate + permanent degraded-mode canary
mechanisms.note: A quality collapse is caught before merge against a committed baseline; degraded mode is a permanent canary.

items.residualGap: Single-agent topology limits cascade scope, but there are no circuit-breakers/bulkheads for a multi-component deployment. Distributed deployments must add failure isolation and backpressure.

items.title: Human-Agent Trust Exploitation
items.summary: Exploiting human over-reliance on agents through misleading explanations, authority misrepresentation, or unwarranted certainty in recommendations.

mechanisms.name: AI Reliability Scorecard (evidence-backed, honest degradation)
mechanisms.note: Board-legible ratings assert no score without traced eval evidence and degrade honestly — directly countering over-trust and authority misrepresentation about the agent's reliability.

mechanisms.name: Validator + portable LLM-as-judge metric
mechanisms.note: Guards against an over-promising draft reply — the Phase 2A finding caught a smaller model over-promising, which the judge metric flags.

items.residualGap: The agent's own output does not surface calibrated uncertainty/confidence to the end user. A deployment relied on for consequential decisions should add explicit uncertainty disclosure and human-in-the-loop confirmation.

items.title: Rogue Agents
items.summary: Agents act beyond intended objectives due to goal drift, collusion, reward hacking, or emergent behavior exceeding designed autonomy boundaries.

mechanisms.name: Enforced turn bound + sole-path gateway
mechanisms.note: The agent cannot act outside the bounded, gateway-guarded channel — autonomy is capped by construction.

mechanisms.name: Eval-gate baseline + degraded canary + OTel traces
mechanisms.note: Behavioral drift is detected against a committed baseline and made inspectable as connected traces (Phase 2D).

items.residualGap: Detection is offline (CI/eval-time) and post-hoc (traces); there is no runtime anomaly detector or kill-switch for emergent behavior in production. High-autonomy deployments should add runtime behavioral monitoring.
